title: Anatomy of a golf hole
microFact: Every hole has the same skeleton: tee box → fairway → green. Everything else exists to punish you for missing them.
lesson:
A hole starts at the TEE BOX (teeing area) — the only place you may put your ball on a tee. The short-mown strip leading to the hole is the FAIRWAY; the longer grass beside it is the ROUGH, which grabs your club and kills distance.

The GREEN is the ultra-short surface around the hole where you putt. The collar of slightly longer grass around it is the FRINGE (or apron). The hole itself is 4.25 inches wide and marked by the FLAGSTICK, often called the PIN.

Scattered along the way: BUNKERS (sand), PENALTY AREAS (water or marked red/yellow zones), and trees. Course designers place these exactly where average golfers hit their misses — which is why course knowledge is worth strokes.

When someone says "the pin's back-left," they mean the hole is cut at the back-left of the green — aim accordingly.

visual.caption: Top-down anatomy of a hole: tee → fairway → green, trouble on the edges.

quiz.question: What is the 'fringe'?
options: The longer grass beside the fairway
The area behind the tee box
The collar of short grass around the green
quiz.explanation: The fringe (or apron) rings the green — shorter than fairway, longer than the putting surface. You can putt from it.
